mnemonics: O.P.T.I.M.I.Z.E. Is Different: Optimization Prefers Training Immediately, Minimizing Ideal Zones Exactly. Learning Doesn't.
Think of a Hiker (Optimization) vs. a Scout (Learning): The hiker takes the quickest path down the mountain. The scout explores, making a better map for future hikers.
Remember the 3 Gs: Goals are different. Optimization has a single, clear Goal. Learning Generalizes for future, unknown Goals.
keyPoints: The goal of optimization is to minimize a specific cost function on training data. The goal of learning is to perform well on unseen test data, which requires generalization.
The training process itself is part of the problem in machine learning. We deal with empirical risk (on training set) instead of the true expected risk (on the entire data distribution).
Learning algorithms must incorporate inductive bias (e.g., via model architecture) to generalize, while pure optimization seeks the single best solution for the given objective.
commonMistakes: Overfitting: Mistaking low training error (good optimization) for successful learning, when the model fails to generalize to new data.
Ignoring the Bias-Variance Trade-off: Using an overly complex model that optimizes the cost function perfectly but has high variance and poor generalization.
Treating the test set as a final optimization target, which leaks information and gives an overly optimistic measure of generalization performance.
